Question: A man in his 50s with a kidney transplant to treat adult polycystic kidney disease, common variable immunodeficiency, and type 2 diabetes mellitus was admitted for a 1-month history of an enlarging left lower extremity mass. He reported that the lesion spontaneously ulcerated, was occasionally painful, and intermittently drained small amounts of sanguinopurulent fluid. He pointed out a similar but smaller ulcerated nodule on the right lower extremity but denied any preceding trauma to the affected areas. His lesions were unresponsive to a course of oral sulfamethaxazole  with trimethoprim, prompting surgical incision and drainage and initiation of intravenous vancomycin and piperacillin with tazobactam therapy. Physical examination revealed an edematous left leg with a 3.5-cm blue-gray tumor with overlying superficial ulceration surrounded by a collarette of delicate scale. Superior to this lesion, there was a firm 7-mm bluish papule. On the right anterior leg, there was a 1.5-cm violaceous nodule with small central ulceration and overlying crust (Figure, A). Deep wound culture of the larger left leg lesion and punch biopsy specimens taken from the larger left leg lesion for histopathological analysis (Figure, B-D) and tissue culture of the right leg lesion were obtained. What Is Your Diagnosis?
Options: A:Cutaneous phaeohyphomycosis B:Pyoderma gangrenosum C:Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus carbuncle D:Nodular Kaposi sarcoma
 Which option is the correct answer to the question? ALWAYS provide the option index of the final answer AT THE END with TRIPLE BACKTICKS among A, B, C, D, EXACTLY like this: The answer is ```X```. DO NOT add any other text or variations.
explanation: Skin biopsy demonstrated pseudoepithelial epidermal hyperplasia and granulomatous dermal inflammation with septate fungal hyphae highlighted by Gomori methanamine silver and Fontana-Masson stains (Figure, C and D). Cultures from lesions on both legs grew black molds identified as Exserohilum species at a reference laboratory. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) without contrast of the lower extremities showed no evidence for deep tissue or bony involvement, and MRI of the brain demonstrated no intracranial lesions concerning for infection. A computed tomographic scan without contrast of the chest demonstrated a 1-cm irregular nodule in the posterior upper lobe and a 6-mm nodule in the lower lobe of the right lung. Cultures and histopathologic evaluation of transbronchial biopsy specimens of the upper lobe and lower lobe nodules were negative for malignancy and fungal infection.Phaeohyphomycosis encompasses a spectrum of dematiaceous fungal infections (>100 species associated with human disease) characterized by the presence of pigmented hyphae due to the deposition of dihydroxynaphthalene melanin in the fungal cell walls.1,2 Phaeohyphomycosis has been reported in immunocompromised and immunocompetent individuals, and the clinical presentation can include superficial or deep cutaneous infections, sinusitis, keratitis, endocarditis, osteomyelitis, meningitis, and/or systemic dissemination.3,4 Subcutaneous infection usually occurs due to traumatic implantation of a fungus by a wooden splinter that the fungus inhabits as a saprophyte.5 McCarty et al6 identified 14 different dematiaceous genera within a transplant population. Alternaria was the most frequent genus (32%), followed by Exophiala (10.7%), and finally 3 cases of Exserohilum infection. A differential diagnosis including phaeohyphomycosis should also include chromoblastomycosis, a localized chronic infection of skin and subcutaneous tissue also caused by dematiaceous fungi. The histopathological characteristics of chromoblastomycosis are brown spores of the fungus, also known as medlar bodies or copper pennies, and the lack of hyphal structures.7Exserohilum, a genus of filamentous opportunistic fungi typically found in grass and soil, is a relatively rare cause of phaeohyphomycosis. Histopathologically, they appear as pale brown, broad septate hyphae with acute branches and are typically found in the dermis encircling blood vessels.8 Although Fontana-Masson staining can aid in diagnosis, it is not required because pigmented fungal organisms can be identified on routinely stained sections. The most common sites of infection are the skin and paranasal sinuses due to direct trauma or inhalation of the pathogen, yet most reported cutaneous infections occurred in patients who were immunocompromised or had other underlying diseases such as malignant conditions, diabetes, neutropenia, malnutrition, or metabolic derangements.2,8 Most recently, Exserohilum rostratum was implicated as the most commonly isolated pathogen during the 2012 multistate outbreak of fungal meningitis related to contaminated methylprednisolone acetate compounded in Framingham, Massachusetts.9 Cutaneous infections are typically responsive to systemic antifungal agents, including amphotericin B and triazoles such as itraconazole and voriconazole, but wide excision of lesional skin may be needed for deeper infections.2,9In the present case, once black mold was identified by culture, treatment with liposomal amphotericin B was initiated. Following the workup to rule out disseminated disease, the patient underwent surgical debridement of his leg lesions, with negative deep and lateral margins found by histopathologic analysis and tissue culture. He was transitioned to oral voriconazole treatment on discharge owing to renal impairment, and his cutaneous lesions had not recurred by 12-month follow-up.
